pattern: Every column can be fixed on its own by walking down it once and pushing each cell up to at least one more than whatever the cell above ended up becoming.
hints: Columns never interact with each other, so solve each column independently and add up the costs.

Walk down a single column from the top, remembering the final value the previous cell was pushed to; if the current cell isn't already bigger than that, the cheapest fix is to raise it to exactly one more.

Track the running 'previous final value' as you go rather than the original cell values -- once you've bumped a cell up, later comparisons in that column need to use its new value, not its original one.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, two columns both need fixes
one column already strictly increasing while the other needs repeated bumps
a column that is strictly decreasing, forcing every row after the first to move
single row, always already valid with no column having more than one entry

stress.description: random grids of varying shape with a mix of small and large values
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = rng.randint(1, 50)
    n = rng.randint(1, 50)
    return [[[rng.randint(0, 2499) for _ in range(n)] for _ in range(m)]]
stress.note: the size parameter is ignored for scale since m and n are already capped at the constraint maximum
